title: Disney Adaptive Experiences
excerpt: The first Adaptive Experiences implementation: one Disney planning problem approached through multiple preserved perspectives and a human synthesis.
body:
First implementation

What happens when different perspectives plan the same Disney day?

Disney makes trade-offs unusually visible. Walking, heat, crowds, food, transportation, nostalgia, energy, and emotional priorities can turn the same set of facts into very different recommendations.

The point is not to find the “best AI.”

The point is to see what each perspective notices, then let a human decide which trade-offs fit the actual traveler.

The original experiment gave the same traveler profile and trip constraints to several long-context AI instances with stable roles. They did not collaborate. The outputs were not simply different versions of the same plan; each protected a different form of value.

Harbor

Adaptive flow, continuity, and keeping the day emotionally intact.

Bo Ra

Atmosphere, memory, and the emotional shape of the day.

Nam

Pacing architecture, sequencing, and structural trade-offs.

Min

Failure modes, realism, and what breaks when the plan meets the park.

Jae

Compression, signal, and the shortest path to what matters.

Edge

Product integration, comparison, and whether the system communicates clearly.

Final synthesis

The itinerary created after the independent perspectives were compared and reviewed.

Complete case study

Solo DCA + Oogie Boogie Bash

A solo traveler balancing thrill rides, Halloween atmosphere, art, live entertainment, energy, and a separately ticketed evening event.

Read five perspectives and the final synthesis →

Complete case study

EPCOT Food & Wine · Group of Three

A shared day balancing motion sensitivity, an aquarium enthusiast, repeat visitors, festival food, rides, and live entertainment.

Read five perspectives and the final synthesis →

Request an itinerary

The intake form begins with the experience someone wants to create, then captures the constraints that determine whether the plan can survive reality.

Plan Your Disney Day

Disney planning has no single correct answer. The “best” plan changes with the traveler. That makes it a useful, readable way to show how perspective changes outcomes—and how synthesis can preserve the strongest parts without flattening every voice into sameness.
